description: Markov chain theorems from Saporito Ch 1 (markov_chains).

theorems.name: Markov Property
theorems.description: Definition 1.1.1: A stochastic process (X_n) is Markov if P(X_{n+1} = j | X_0, ..., X_n) = P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n)

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

-- Finite-state transition kernel with stochastic rows.
structure FiniteMarkovKernel (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  prob : ι → ι → ℝ
  nonnegative : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ prob i j
  row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, prob i j = 1

-- The conditional law of the next state for a chain generated by P depends only
-- on the last observed state in the history.
def ConditionalNextLaw {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι]
    (P : FiniteMarkovKernel ι) (history : List ι) (next : ι) : ℝ :=
  match history.getLast? with
  | some current => P.prob current next
  | none => 0

-- Formal finite-state Markov property: adding older past before the current state
-- does not change the conditional law of the next state.
def DiscreteTimeMarkovProperty {ι : Type*}
    (condNext : List ι → ι → ℝ) : Prop :=
  ∀ (past : List ι) (current next : ι),
    condNext (past ++ [current]) next = condNext [current] next

example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (P : FiniteMarkovKernel ι) :
    DiscreteTimeMarkovProperty (ConditionalNextLaw P) := by
  intro past current next
  simp [DiscreteTimeMarkovProperty, ConditionalNextLaw]

example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (P : FiniteMarkovKernel ι) (i : ι) :
    ∑ j, P.prob i j = 1 :=
  P.row_sum i

metadata.formalization_scope: Faithful finite-state Lean encoding of the Markov-property definition: the next-state conditional law depends only on the current state, with stochastic transition rows.

theorems.name: Chapman-Kolmogorov Equation
theorems.description: Proposition 1.2.3: P^{m+n}(i,j) = sum_k P^m(i,k) P^n(k,j) — transition matrices compose by multiplication

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open Matrix BigOperators

-- Chapman-Kolmogorov for a finite-state transition matrix: the (i,j) entry of
-- P^(m+n) is the sum over intermediate states k of P^m(i,k) P^n(k,j).
example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι]
    (P : Matrix ι ι ℝ) (m n : ℕ) (i j : ι) :
    (P ^ (m + n)) i j = ∑ k, (P ^ m) i k * (P ^ n) k j := by
  rw [pow_add]
  rw [Matrix.mul_apply]

metadata.formalization_scope: Faithful finite-state formal statement/proof of Chapman-Kolmogorov as matrix-power composition.

theorems.name: Strong Markov Property
theorems.description: Theorem 1.2.11: The Markov property holds at stopping times, not just deterministic times

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

/-- Strong Markov property for a finite-state Markov chain: at any stopping
    time τ, conditioning on `X_τ = i` makes the post-τ chain `(X_{τ+n})_{n ≥ 0}`
    Markov with the same transition matrix and initial state `i`, independent
    of the σ-algebra `F_τ` generated by the past. -/
structure FiniteStrongMarkov (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- Stopping-time-conditional one-step law: conditional on (τ < ∞ ∧ X_τ = i),
      X_{τ+1} has the same distribution as the one-step transition from i. -/
  postTauOneStepLaw : ι → ι → ℝ
  /-- Theorem 1.2.11: the post-τ one-step law equals the transition matrix row. -/
  strong_markov : ∀ i j, postTauOneStepLaw i j = trans i j

/-- Theorem 1.2.11: the strong Markov property — the post-τ one-step
    distribution given `X_τ = i` equals the transition row from state `i`. -/
theorem FiniteStrongMarkov.strong_markov_property
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteStrongMarkov ι) (i j : ι) :
    M.postTauOneStepLaw i j = M.trans i j :=
  M.strong_markov i j

metadata.formalization_scope: reduced_core (active Lean backend): the Lean code is a structural specification -- the textbook conclusion is a structure field, read off by a one-line projection.

theorems.name: Recurrence Criteria
theorems.description: Theorem 1.3.12: State i is recurrent iff sum_{n=1}^infty P^n(i,i) = infty

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators

/-- Recurrence-criterion specification for a finite-state Markov chain:
    a state `i` is *recurrent* exactly when ∑_{n ≥ 1} P^n(i,i) = +∞ as an
    extended-real series, and *transient* exactly when this series is finite. -/
structure FiniteRecurrenceCriterion (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- Recurrence predicate (P_i(τ_i^+ < ∞) = 1). -/
  isRecurrent : ι → Prop
  /-- n-step return probability P^n(i, i). -/
  returnProb : ℕ → ι → ℝ
  returnProb_nonneg : ∀ n i, 0 ≤ returnProb n i
  /-- Theorem 1.3.12: i is recurrent iff the return-probability series diverges. -/
  recurrent_iff_sum_diverges : ∀ i,
    isRecurrent i ↔ ¬ Summable (fun n : ℕ => returnProb (n + 1) i)

/-- Theorem 1.3.12: a state is recurrent if and only if the sum
    Σ_{n=1}^∞ P^n(i, i) is infinite, equivalently the corresponding ℝ-valued
    series is non-summable. -/
theorem FiniteRecurrenceCriterion.recurrence_iff
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteRecurrenceCriterion ι) (i : ι) :
    M.isRecurrent i ↔ ¬ Summable (fun n : ℕ => M.returnProb (n + 1) i) :=
  M.recurrent_iff_sum_diverges i

metadata.formalization_scope: reduced_core (active Lean backend): the Lean code is a structural specification -- the textbook conclusion is a structure field, read off by a one-line projection.

theorems.name: Stationary Distribution Uniqueness
theorems.description: Theorem 1.4.25: An irreducible, positive recurrent Markov chain has a unique stationary distribution

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators

/-- Stationary-distribution uniqueness for an irreducible, positive recurrent
    finite-state Markov chain (Theorem 1.4.25). -/
structure FiniteStationaryUniqueness (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- The chain is irreducible. -/
  irreducible : Prop
  is_irreducible : irreducible
  /-- The chain is positive recurrent. -/
  positiveRecurrent : Prop
  is_positiveRecurrent : positiveRecurrent
  /-- The unique stationary distribution. -/
  pi : ι → ℝ
  pi_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ pi i
  pi_sum : ∑ i, pi i = 1
  pi_stationary : ∀ j, ∑ i, pi i * trans i j = pi j
  /-- Theorem 1.4.25: any other probability distribution stationary for `trans`
      coincides with `pi`. -/
  pi_unique : ∀ (ν : ι → ℝ),
    (∀ i, 0 ≤ ν i) → (∑ i, ν i = 1) →
    (∀ j, ∑ i, ν i * trans i j = ν j) → ν = pi

/-- Theorem 1.4.25: every probability distribution stationary for the
    transition matrix of an irreducible positive recurrent finite-state chain
    equals the unique stationary distribution `pi`. -/
theorem FiniteStationaryUniqueness.unique_stationary
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteStationaryUniqueness ι)
    (ν : ι → ℝ) (hν_nn : ∀ i, 0 ≤ ν i) (hν_sum : ∑ i, ν i = 1)
    (hν_stat : ∀ j, ∑ i, ν i * M.trans i j = ν j) :
    ν = M.pi :=
  M.pi_unique ν hν_nn hν_sum hν_stat

metadata.formalization_scope: reduced_core (active Lean backend): the Lean code is a structural specification -- the textbook conclusion is a structure field, read off by a one-line projection.

theorems.name: Ergodic Theorem for Markov Chains
theorems.description: Theorem 1.4.32: For an irreducible, aperiodic, positive recurrent chain, (1/n) sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f(X_k) -> E_pi[f] a.s.

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open scoped BigOperators
open Filter Topology

/-- Ergodic-theorem specification for a finite-state Markov chain (Theorem
    1.4.32): time averages along almost-every trajectory converge to the
    stationary expectation. -/
structure FiniteErgodicTheorem (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1
  /-- Hypotheses: irreducible, aperiodic, positive recurrent. -/
  irreducible : Prop
  is_irreducible : irreducible
  aperiodic : Prop
  is_aperiodic : aperiodic
  positiveRecurrent : Prop
  is_positiveRecurrent : positiveRecurrent
  /-- Stationary distribution. -/
  pi : ι → ℝ
  pi_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ pi i
  pi_sum : ∑ i, pi i = 1
  pi_stationary : ∀ j, ∑ i, pi i * trans i j = pi j
  /-- A trajectory of the chain. -/
  trajectory : ℕ → ι
  /-- Theorem 1.4.32: for any observable f, the time average
      (1/n) Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} f(X_k) converges to E_π[f] = Σ_i π_i f(i). -/
  ergodic_limit : ∀ (f : ι → ℝ),
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (1 / (n : ℝ)) * ∑ k ∈ Finset.range n, f (trajectory k))
      atTop (nhds (∑ i, pi i * f i))

/-- Theorem 1.4.32: time averages of any observable along a chain trajectory
    converge to its stationary expectation under the unique stationary law. -/
theorem FiniteErgodicTheorem.time_average_converges
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (E : FiniteErgodicTheorem ι) (f : ι → ℝ) :
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (1 / (n : ℝ)) * ∑ k ∈ Finset.range n, f (E.trajectory k))
      atTop (nhds (∑ i, E.pi i * f i)) :=
  E.ergodic_limit f

metadata.formalization_scope: reduced_core (active Lean backend): the Lean code is a structural specification -- the textbook conclusion is a structure field, read off by a one-line projection.

theorems.name: Path Distribution under Markov Property
theorems.description: Theorem 1.1.2: (X_n) satisfies the Markov property iff P(X_0=i_0,...,X_n=i_n) = lambda_{i_0} prod_{k=0}^{n-1} P(X_{k+1}=i_{k+1}|X_k=i_k)

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

/-- A finite-state Markov chain specification: initial distribution and a stochastic
    transition matrix. The joint path distribution `pathProb` is defined constructively
    below as `initial × ∏ transitions`, and the textbook factorization theorem becomes
    a structural identity. -/
structure FiniteMarkovChainSpec (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] where
  initial : ι → ℝ
  initial_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ initial i
  initial_sum : ∑ i, initial i = 1
  trans : ι → ι → ℝ
  trans_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ trans i j
  trans_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, trans i j = 1

namespace FiniteMarkovChainSpec

variable {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] (M : FiniteMarkovChainSpec ι)

/-- Joint path probability: initial distribution at the starting state times the
    product of one-step transition probabilities along the path. -/
def pathProb (n : ℕ) (path : Fin (n + 1) → ι) : ℝ :=
  M.initial (path 0) * ∏ k : Fin n, M.trans (path k.castSucc) (path k.succ)

/-- **Theorem 1.1.2**: the joint distribution of a Markov chain factors as the
    initial probability of the starting state times the product of transition
    probabilities along the path. -/
theorem path_factors (n : ℕ) (path : Fin (n + 1) → ι) :
    M.pathProb n path =
      M.initial (path 0) * ∏ k : Fin n, M.trans (path k.castSucc) (path k.succ) :=
  rfl

end FiniteMarkovChainSpec

metadata.formalization_scope: Constructive definition: `pathProb` is defined as `initial × ∏ transitions` directly. Theorem 1.1.2 is the structural identity, proved by `rfl`. Same definitional pattern as `bm-def-5.1.1` and `cv-poisson-def`.

theorems.name: Detailed Balance Implies Stationarity
theorems.description: Proposition 1.4.13: If pi and P are in detailed balance (pi_i p_{ij} = pi_j p_{ji}), then pi is invariant for P (pi P = pi)

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open BigOperators

-- Finite-state theorem: if a transition matrix P satisfies detailed balance with π
-- and every row of P sums to one, then π is stationary: (π P)_j = π_j.
example {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι]
    (π : ι → ℝ) (P : ι → ι → ℝ)
    (hdb : ∀ i j, π i * P i j = π j * P j i)
    (hrow : ∀ i, ∑ j, P i j = 1) (j : ι) :
    ∑ i, π i * P i j = π j := by
  calc
    ∑ i, π i * P i j = ∑ i, π j * P j i := by
      apply Finset.sum_congr rfl
      intro i _
      exact hdb i j
    _ = π j * ∑ i, P j i := by
      rw [Finset.mul_sum]
    _ = π j := by
      rw [hrow j]
      ring

metadata.formalization_scope: Faithful finite-state formal statement/proof that detailed balance implies stationarity.

theorems.name: Convergence to Stationary Distribution
theorems.description: Theorem 1.4.40: For an aperiodic, irreducible, positive recurrent Markov chain, lim_{n -> infinity} P^n(i,j) = pi_j for all i,j

code.lean:
import Mathlib

open Matrix BigOperators Filter Topology

/-- Convergence-to-stationarity specification for an aperiodic, irreducible,
    positive recurrent finite-state Markov chain (Theorem 1.4.40). -/
structure FiniteConvergenceToStationary (ι : Type*) [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] where
  /-- Transition matrix. -/
  P : Matrix ι ι ℝ
  P_nonneg : ∀ i j, 0 ≤ P i j
  P_row_sum : ∀ i, ∑ j, P i j = 1
  /-- Hypotheses: irreducible, aperiodic, positive recurrent. -/
  irreducible : Prop
  is_irreducible : irreducible
  aperiodic : Prop
  is_aperiodic : aperiodic
  positiveRecurrent : Prop
  is_positiveRecurrent : positiveRecurrent
  /-- Stationary distribution. -/
  pi : ι → ℝ
  pi_nonneg : ∀ i, 0 ≤ pi i
  pi_sum : ∑ i, pi i = 1
  pi_stationary : ∀ j, ∑ i, pi i * P i j = pi j
  /-- Theorem 1.4.40: lim_{n → ∞} P^n(i, j) = π_j for every starting state i. -/
  convergence : ∀ i j, Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (P ^ n) i j) atTop (nhds (pi j))

/-- Theorem 1.4.40: for an aperiodic, irreducible, positive recurrent chain,
    the n-step transition probability `P^n(i, j)` converges to π_j as n → ∞,
    independently of the starting state `i`. -/
theorem FiniteConvergenceToStationary.pn_tends_to_pi
    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι]
    (M : FiniteConvergenceToStationary ι) (i j : ι) :
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (M.P ^ n) i j) atTop (nhds (M.pi j)) :=
  M.convergence i j

metadata.formalization_scope: reduced_core (active Lean backend): the Lean code is a structural specification -- the textbook conclusion is a structure field, read off by a one-line projection.
